The sound of Africa is everywhere.
Two licences. One placement. Most composers only know about one.
If you've ever wondered why sync deals feel complicated — every placement requires two separate legal permissions.
🔑 THE SYNC LICENCE | Permission to pair your composition with visual content.
Controlled by: the publisher (or you, if you self-publish).
🔑 THE MASTER LICENCE | Permission to use the specific recording.
Controlled by: whoever owns the master — often the label, unless you recorded independently. · One placement = two separate negotiations.
Here's what you need to know:
If a label owns your master: · They share in every sync deal you ever make · A supervisor has to negotiate with two parties · That friction kills deals
Supervisors prioritise one-stop tracks. | Fewer calls. Faster deals. No surprises.

Education · Why your SoundCloud link isn't enough
Sending a SoundCloud link to a music supervisor is like sending a screenshot of
your CV to a hiring manager.
Here's what a supervisor actually needs to move your track forward:
It tells them you exist. That's all. ↓
✓ High-quality WAV or AIFF | Not a stream.
Not a download link. A file, ready immediately.
✓ Embedded metadata | Title, composer, ISRC, mood, genre, BPM — inside the file,
✓ Rights status confirmed | One-stop or clearly defined split, documented before
you pitch. · ✓ Stems available | Not now. But ready when they ask.
✓ Response within 24 hours | If you respond in 3 days, the slot is filled.
Here's what you need to know:
SoundCloud tells a supervisor: · A track exists · You have a profile · That's not enough to move a placement forward.

Behind the Scenes · Anatomy of a sync brief
Orchestral. Afrofusion influence. Hopeful. No vocals. One-stop only." · Every word in a sync brief is a specification. Not a suggestion. ↓
🎻 ORCHESTRAL | Real strings or a convincing orchestral arrangement.
Bedroom synth pads don't make the shortlist here.
🌍 AFROFUSION INFLUENCE | The tonal and rhythmic language of African music — not pastiche, not performed.
Not triumphant. Hopeful is quieter, more complex. The distinction matters enormously to the picture edit.
🚫 NO VOCALS | Not even background vocals.
Not even a hum. Fully instrumental or the track is out.
✅ ONE-STOP ONLY | You control both master and publishing.
If you need a label involved, the track is disqualified.

Here's the detailed feedback we gave the 14 who didn't make it — because rejection without explanation is useless.
The 14 we didn't accept were rejected for readiness, not music.
Here's what you need to know:
9/14 had rights issues · Labels holding masters with no clear route to clearance · Uncleared samples embedded in commercial-quality work · Publishing registered with the wrong entity
Here's what you need to know:
8/14 had metadata problems · No ISRC codes assigned · No BPM, key, or mood data · Files with no embedded information
Here's what you need to know:
6/14 had file issues · Only MP3 available · No stems exported or accessible · Files not named to any standard
2/14 had response issues | → 3+ day reply time in the response-time test scenario
Most of these overlap. Most are fixable.

Your track has no ISRC code?
That's the first thing we fix at SyncMaster.
Here's exactly why it matters more than almost anything else in your catalogue. ↓ · ISRC = International Standard Recording Code
A unique identifier assigned to each individual recording.
Think of it as a barcode for your music. · Why it matters for sync specifically:
Here's what you need to know:

It identifies your recording in global licensing databases · It links your track to royalty collection societies worldwide (SAMRO, COSON, SOCAN, PRS, ASCAP, and more) · Without it, performance royalties from any placement can't reach you · Without it, your track can't be properly catalogued in sync libraries
A placement without an ISRC = money you'll never collect.
Here's what you need to know:
Through your national music body: · RISA (South Africa) · COSON (Nigeria) · MCSK (Kenya)
Through your distributor: | → DistroKid, TuneCore, CD Baby all assign ISRCs
Cost: Free or minimal. | Time: 24–72 hours in most cases.
Every track in the SyncMaster catalogue has an ISRC.
